flashcards.front: What is the Many-to-One multithreading model?
flashcards.back: A model where multiple user-level threads are mapped to a single kernel thread. All thread management is done in user space by the thread library.

flashcards.front: What is the One-to-One multithreading model?
flashcards.back: A model where each user-level thread is mapped to a separate kernel thread. Provides greater concurrency but limited by system resources.

flashcards.front: What is the Many-to-Many multithreading model?
flashcards.back: A model where many user-level threads are multiplexed to an equal or lesser number of kernel threads, combining benefits of both models.
A collection of pre-created worker threads that are ready to execute tasks, reducing the overhead of creating new threads for each task.

flashcards.front: What is the Two-Level multithreading model?
flashcards.back: A variation of Many-to-Many where some user threads are bound to dedicated kernel threads while others are multiplexed.

flashcards.front: What are the advantages of the Many-to-One model?
flashcards.back: Simple implementation, efficient for applications that never block (pure compute-bound), no kernel support required.

flashcards.front: What is the main limitation of the Many-to-One model?
flashcards.back: If one user thread blocks (e.g., for I/O), the entire process blocks because only one kernel thread exists.

flashcards.front: Why is the One-to-One model called 1:1 model?
flashcards.back: Because every user thread created by the application corresponds to exactly one kernel thread managed by the OS.

flashcards.front: What is thread scheduling in user space?
flashcards.back: When the thread library manages thread scheduling without kernel involvement, using the Many-to-One model.

flashcards.front: What is thread scheduling in kernel space?
flashcards.back: When the OS kernel directly schedules threads, as in One-to-One and Many-to-Many models.
A technique where a fixed number of threads are created at startup and reused to execute multiple tasks, improving performance.
Threads simulated at the user level (not by the OS), used in early Java and some language implementations.

flashcards.front: What is the main advantage of the Many-to-Many model?
flashcards.back: It allows the number of kernel threads to be optimized for the application and system, providing both concurrency and scalability.

flashcards.front: What happens in a thread library when a thread invokes a blocking system call?
flashcards.back: In Many-to-One model, the entire process blocks. In One-to-One, only that thread blocks while others continue.

flashcards.front: What is thread concurrency versus parallelism?
flashcards.back: Concurrency is when multiple threads make progress over overlapping time periods. Parallelism is when multiple threads execute simultaneously on multiple processors.
